retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Evaluation -- Exhaled Nitric Oxide
retrieved_docs.content: Eosinophilic airway inflammation causes an upregulation of nitric oxide synthase in the respiratory mucosa and increased nitric oxide levels in the exhaled breath. The fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FE NO ) levels in some patients with asthma are higher than those without asthma. FE NO is a noninvasive biomarker that indicates the presence of asthma and eosinophilic airway inflammation. A FE NO of less than 25 ppb in adults and less than 20 ppb in children aged 12 or younger implies the absence of eosinophilic airway inflammation. A FE NO greater than 35 ppb in children suggests eosinophilic airway inflammation. The exact role of FE NO measurement in diagnosing and managing asthma is undefined.

retrieved_docs.title: Bullous Emphysema -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: To evaluate for other potential etiologies of dyspnea, patients should undergo a complete blood count, serum electrolytes and kidney function, thyroid stimulating hormone level, and plasma brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) or N-terminal pro-BNP to assess for anemia, kidney disease, thyroid disorders, or heart failure.  Electrocardiography may show right axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy, or right atrial hypertrophy. Expected findings due to COPD may be increased hemoglobin and hematocrit due to a reactive erythrocytosis secondary to chronic hypoxia. In addition, eosinophilia can indicate a type II inflammation phenotype and overlap with asthma. [26] The eosinophil count can serve as a potential biomarker and assist in stratifying patients into treatable groups. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Status Asthmaticus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory tests and imaging help identify treatable asthma triggers, assess disease severity, and evaluate alternative diagnoses. Initial blood tests may include a complete blood count (CBC), basic metabolic panel (BMP), and arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis. In status asthmaticus, CBC may show leukocytosis from inflammation with neutrophil demargination or superimposed infection. Marked neutrophilia is often associated with bacterial infection. Viral infections may present with leukocytosis or leukopenia, accompanied by lymphocytosis or mild lymphopenia. Infection may be confirmed with a nasopharyngeal swab, sputum test, or blood culture, depending on the suspected organism.

retrieved_docs.title: Nonspecific Orbital Inflammation -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory evaluation usually includes hematologic work-up, routine biochemistry, inflammatory markers, and a comprehensive autoimmune panel. A hematologic work-up consists of a complete blood cell count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, electrolytes, thyroid function studies, sedimentation rate, antinuclear antibodies, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, rheumatoid factor, angiotensin-converting enzyme level, and rapid plasma regain test. [5] [53] Active disease activity can be assessed by inflammatory markers such as soluble interleukin-2 receptor (sIL-2R) and interleukin-6 (IL-6). [54] While laboratory findings within normal limits yield little diagnostic value, they are necessary to exclude other causes.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Treatment / Management -- Conventional Drugs that Combat Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids also prevent several mechanisms involved in inflammation. Glucocorticoids are prescribed for several inflammatory conditions including inflammatory arthritis, systemic lupus, sarcoidosis, and asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Cough -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The present guidelines offer recommendations for utilizing noninvasive techniques for measuring airway inflammation when evaluating and managing cough associated with asthma or NAEB, based on a thorough review of the pertinent literature. [56] Evidence for ongoing airway eosinophilic inflammation can be sought by performing differential cell counts on samples from sputum induction or bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL). In such cases, elevated eosinophils (>3%) in the airways without bronchial hyperresponsiveness would suggest eosinophilic bronchitis, reported in up to 13% of patients attending cough clinics. [57] The usefulness of fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) or blood eosinophils in diagnosing or predicting treatment response in patients with chronic cough has yet to be fully assessed in a clinical setting. Despite this, there is currently insufficient high-quality evidence available. Controlled trials with a placebo group are necessary to determine efficacy, and consensus regarding the threshold levels for patients with chronic cough is needed. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Eosinophilic Granulomatosis With Polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss Syndrome) -- Evaluation -- Other Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral blood eosinophilia (>10% on differential white blood cell count or >1500 cells/dL) is a well-recognized lab hallmark of the disease. Elevated serum IgE is also observed in about 75% of patients. Other laboratory abnormalities are nonspecific and may include elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), normocytic normochromic anemia, and extrapulmonary rheumatoid factor in approximately 60% of patients. [7] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergic and Environmentally Induced Asthma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Biomarkers of inflammation are being evaluated for their usefulness in the diagnosis of asthma. Biomarkers include total and differential cell count and mediator assays in sputum, blood, urine, and exhaled air.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Issues of Concern -- Asthma Phenotype
retrieved_docs.content: Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) guidelines show that population-level recommendations depend on type II or non-type II phenotypes. Type II inflammation is found in patients with severe asthma and atopy. It is associated with allergic conditions such as nasal polyposis and atopic dermatitis. Elevated serum, sputum eosinophils, and increased FeNO characterize type 2 inflammation. Management includes optimal use of ICS and targeted biologics. Dupilumab is indicated in patients with severe eosinophilia or type 2 asthma uncontrolled on high-dose ICS-LABA. Pediatric patients with persistently elevated IgE levels respond well to omalizumab. [49] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Allergic asthma: A complex, chronic inflammatory disorder associated with inappropriate immune response and inflammation in conducting airways involving a decline in airway function and tissue remodeling.

retrieved_docs.title: Status Asthmaticus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Inhalational injury may precipitate or mimic an asthma exacerbation due to airway inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Evaluation -- Tests for Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, there are no highly effective laboratory measures to assess patients for chronic inflammation and diagnoses are only undertaken when the inflammation occurs in association with another medical condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Status Asthmaticus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Acute asthma consists of 2 physiological phases. The early bronchospastic phase occurs minutes after allergen or trigger exposure, involving mast cell degranulation and release of inflammatory mediators such as histamine, prostaglandin D2, and leukotriene C4. The latter inflammatory phase results in airway edema due to eosinophilic release of cationic and major basic proteins. [12] Type 2 inflammation appears to increase the risk of acute asthma exacerbation. This process involves the release of interleukins 4, 5, and 13 from T-helper type 2 cells and type 2 innate lymphoid cells. Non-type 2 inflammation, characterized by neutrophil involvement, also likely plays a significant role in asthma exacerbations. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Cough -- Etiology -- Nonasthmatic Eosinophilic Bronchitis
retrieved_docs.content: NAEB is characterized by eosinophilic inflammation in the airways, similar to asthma. However, it lacks the variable airflow obstruction characteristic of asthma. [9] There is no verifiable airflow limitation on spirometry in NAEB, and methacholine challenge testing (MCT) is usually negative. [10] Chest x-ray results are typically normal. The lack of airflow limitation on spirometry distinguishes NAEB from asthma. In NAEB, sputum examination can reveal eosinophilia in the airways. Unlike asthma, there is usually no mast-cell infiltration in eosinophilic bronchitis. [11] Patients with recurrent episodes of symptomatic NAEB are at an increased risk of developing asthma, and chronic airway obstruction suggests that NAEB may represent an early stage or precursor to asthma in some individuals. This supports the notion that there might be a spectrum of airway diseases, and various factors could influence the progression from eosinophilic bronchitis to asthma. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑asthmatic therapy involves the corticosteroid beclomethasone, which is classified as an inhaled steroid agent (ISA) and is used to manage airway disease. Beclomethasone specifically targets inflammation (الالتهاب), reducing inflammatory processes in the respiratory tract and thereby improving asthma control. This highlights beclomethasone’s central role as an anti‑asthmatic medication that treats inflammatory airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
retrieved_docs.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: swelling and bronchoconstriction, highlighting its potential therapeutic role in histamine‑mediated conditions. This taxonomy clarifies systemic versus inhaled routes, adrenergic subtypes, and the composition of combination bronchodilator products, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for optimal management of obstructive airway diseases and related allergic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease constitute a therapeutic class that includes ciclesonide and flunisolide as member agents. Within this class, several related glucocorticoid compounds are identified as child entities, namely dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. These inhaled glucocorticoids are employed to reduce inflammation and improve airflow in patients with obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. In addition to their inhaled applications, dexamethasone acetate has been recognized for its potential role in the management of severe systemic inflammation, exemplified by its use in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of glucocorticoid therapy, ranging from targeted inhalation for airway disease to broader systemic anti‑inflammatory interventions.

This classification groups the two drugs together within the same therapeutic class, highlighting their shared role in managing airway inflammation and improving respiratory outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1845
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease comprise a therapeutic group of inhaled corticosteroids used to manage conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Within this group, Momesalic and flunisolide are identified as member agents, while prednisolone sodium succinate and triamcinolone diacetate are considered child compounds that belong to the same class. All of these agents share the common mechanism of delivering glucocorticoid activity directly to the airways, thereby reducing inflammation and improving airflow in patients with obstructive airway disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1608
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease constitute a therapeutic class of anti‑inflammatory agents used to manage conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Within this class, flunisolide is identified as a member, indicating that it belongs to the group of inhaled glucocorticoids indicated for obstructive airway disease. Additionally, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate are described as child compounds of the same class, reflecting that they are specific glucocorticoid formulations that fall under the broader category of inhaled agents for treating obstructive airway disease. All four agents share the common mechanism of reducing airway inflammation, thereby improving respiratory function and symptom control in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 482

Within this class, flunisolide is identified as a member, while dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate are described as child or sub‑type compounds of the same group. In addition to their inhaled application for obstructive airway disease, dexamethasone acetate is also noted for its potential use in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة).
